question: Explain the difference between attitudinal and behavioral measures in UX research.
expectedPoints: Attitudinal measures capture self-reported data about user opinions, beliefs, and feelings
Behavioral measures capture objective observations of actual user actions like clicks, navigation patterns, and task completion
Attitudinal is what users say they think; Behavioral is what users actually do
Both are complementary and provide different insights into user experience

question: Describe the System Usability Scale (SUS) and its interpretation.
expectedPoints: SUS is a 10-question standardized questionnaire measuring perceived usability
Scores range from 0-100 with 68 considered average
Questions alternate between positive and negative phrasing
Scores above 68 indicate above-average usability; above 80 is excellent
Provides quick, reliable measure of perceived usability for comparison

question: What is Net Promoter Score (NPS) and how is it calculated?
expectedPoints: NPS measures customer loyalty based on recommendation likelihood
Users rate likelihood to recommend on 0-10 scale
Promoters: 9-10, Passives: 7-8, Detractors: 0-6
Formula: %Promoters - %Detractors
Score ranges from -100 to +100

question: List and explain three key usability metrics used in UI/UX measurement.
expectedPoints: Task Completion Time - measures efficiency by recording time to complete tasks
Error Rate - measures effectiveness by counting user mistakes during task completion
Success Rate - measures effectiveness by calculating percentage of tasks completed successfully
User Satisfaction - measured through surveys like CSAT or SEQ
Click Depth - measures efficiency by counting pages/clicks needed to complete tasks

question: Explain the Think-Aloud Protocol as a usability evaluation method.
expectedPoints: Think-Aloud is a qualitative method where users verbalize thoughts while performing tasks
Provides insight into user's mental model and decision-making process
Researchers record verbalizations to identify confusion points and expectations
Helps understand why users make certain decisions or mistakes
Can be conducted in-lab or remotely with screen recording

question: What is A/B Testing and what are its advantages in UI/UX design?
answer: A/B Testing is a comparative method where two versions of a design are tested with different user groups to determine which performs better based on predefined metrics like conversion rates or task success.
expectedPoints: A/B testing compares two versions (A and B) of a design
Users are randomly assigned to either version
Metrics measured include conversion rates, task completion, time on page
Provides data-driven evidence for design decisions
Reduces risk by testing changes before full implementation
Can measure real user behavior in natural environment

question: Discuss Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics and explain each briefly with examples.
expectedPoints: Visibility of system status - keep users informed (loading indicators)
Match between system and real world - use familiar language
User control and freedom - undo/redo, back buttons
Consistency and standards - follow platform conventions
Error prevention - confirmation dialogs for destructive actions
Recognition rather than recall - show options, don't require memorization
Flexibility and efficiency of use - shortcuts for experts
Aesthetic and minimalist design - remove irrelevant information
Help users recognize errors - clear error messages with solutions
Help and documentation - searchable help section

question: Design a comprehensive usability testing plan for a mobile banking app. Include metrics, instruments, and procedures.
expectedPoints: Define objectives - evaluate fund transfer functionality
Select participants - target users (10-15 representative users)
Choose metrics - task completion time, success rate, error rate, SUS, SEQ
Prepare test tasks - complete fund transfer, view transaction history, pay bills
Select instruments - think-aloud protocol, post-task questionnaires, system usability scale
Procedure - welcome, consent, demographic questions, task execution, debrief
Data analysis - calculate metrics, identify patterns, prioritize issues
Reporting - document findings with severity ratings and recommendations

question: Explain different types of rating scales used in UX research with advantages and disadvantages.
expectedPoints: Likert Scale - 5/7 point agreement scale, easy to use but may have central tendency bias
Semantic Differential - bipolar adjectives, good for attitude measurement but interpretation varies
Numerical Rating Scale (NRS) - 0-10 scale, simple but lacks verbal anchors
Net Promoter Score - single question for loyalty, industry standard but limited insight
Guttman Scale - cumulative, measures intensity but complex to construct
Thurstone Scale - equal interval, scientifically rigorous but time-consuming to develop

question: How would you measure the ROI (Return on Investment) of UX improvements in a product? Explain with an example.
expectedPoints: Define baseline metrics before improvements - task completion rate, error rate, customer support tickets, conversion rate
Implement UX changes and measure post-improvement metrics
Calculate ROI formula: (Revenue - Cost of UX Investment) / Cost of UX Investment × 100
Example: Reducing form fields from 10 to 5 increased conversion by 20%, generating ₹10 lakhs additional revenue with ₹2 lakhs investment = 400% ROI
Consider both quantitative (conversion, revenue) and qualitative (satisfaction, brand perception) factors
Track metrics over time to measure sustained impact
